coredata.dc:title: Synthesis and characterization of halloysite/graphene quantum dots magnetic nanocomposite as a new adsorbent for Pb(II) removal from water
coredata.dc:creator: Zare Pirhaji, Jamileh
Moeinpour, Farid
Mirhoseini Dehabadi, Abolghasem
Yasini Ardakani, Seyed Ali
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were covalently immobilized onto the NiFe2O4-halloysite nanotubes (NiFe2O4-HNTs) surface to fabricate a nanocomposite material utilized as an active adsorbent to eliminate Pb(II) ions from water. NiFe2O4 nanoparticles were synthesized and simultaneously deposited on HNTs. Then, the material surface was coated by APTES (aminopropyltriethoxysilane) to cause GQDs be connected to the external layer via an amide bond. The prepared nanomaterial structure was identified by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR), Brunauer-Emmet-Teller (BET) isotherm, energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) analysis and VSM (vibrating sample magnetometry). Box-Behnken design incorporated with response surface method (RSM) was utilized to find out the impact of pH, time, initial concentration of Pb(II) and adsorbent dose on lead removal. Study discovered that adsorption operation is the quasi-second-order kinetic model and adapted more precisely with Langmuir adsorption model. The Langmuir highest uptake capacity of 42.02 mg/g at 298 K was acquired. The Elovich model recommended that the process is a kind of chemisorption. The calculated thermodynamic variables verified that the uptake process is endothermic and spontaneous. Additionally, the adsorbent can be simply separated with the aid of a magnet.

head.ce:title: Synthesis and characterization of halloysite/graphene quantum dots magnetic nanocomposite as a new adsorbent for Pb(II) removal from water

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were covalently immobilized onto the NiFe2O4-halloysite nanotubes (NiFe2O4-HNTs) surface to fabricate a nanocomposite material utilized as an active adsorbent to eliminate Pb(II) ions from water. NiFe2O4 nanoparticles were synthesized and simultaneously deposited on HNTs. Then, the material surface was coated by APTES (aminopropyltriethoxysilane) to cause GQDs be connected to the external layer via an amide bond. The prepared nanomaterial structure was identified by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR), Brunauer-Emmet-Teller (BET) isotherm, energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) analysis and VSM (vibrating sample magnetometry). Box-Behnken design incorporated with response surface method (RSM) was utilized to find out the impact of pH, time, initial concentration of Pb(II) and adsorbent dose on lead removal. Study discovered that adsorption operation is the quasi-second-order kinetic model and adapted more precisely with Langmuir adsorption model. The Langmuir highest uptake capacity of 42.02 mg/g at 298 K was acquired. The Elovich model recommended that the process is a kind of chemisorption. The calculated thermodynamic variables verified that the uptake process is endothermic and spontaneous. Additionally, the adsorbent can be simply separated with the aid of a magnet.

ce:section.ce:para: There is a very close correlation within the progress of industry and technology and the pollution. Industrial development and ignoring the environmental considerations are among the most fundamental environmental degradation factors. The wastewater polluted with toxic metal species, even at the acceptable level, when inserted in the environment and affected by other physical, chemical and microbial agents, has harmful effects on the environment irresistibly [1]. Lead belonged to the dangerous heavy metal category [2], which is found in the waste water most often in the form of Pb2+ ions, which is very stable in nature. Lead mainly affects neural, circulatory, digestive, kidney, cardiovascular and reproductive systems [3]. This metal is mainly of industrial origin, and it is introduced into the environment by various industries such as battery-making, painting, photographed, piping, refineries, water treatment, pharmacy and so on [4,5]. In the plating industry, in accordance to the type of plating, lead concentration was reported approximately 3 to 10 mg/L [6]. Today, elimination of toxic metal species from wastewater has become a significant issue. The main reasons are the stringent laws of many countries to control water pollution [2]. The United States Environmental Protection Agency (U.S. EPA) has announced the maximum permissible concentration of lead in sewage and drinking water, respectively, 0.1 and 0.015 mg/L (standard no. EPA 822-F-18-001) [7]. With regard to the above mentioned, it is impossible to raise the world's fresh water resources to solve this crisis; The only action to be taken is to improve its use. Up to now, very much techniques such as co-precipitation, oxidation, electrochemical processes, reverse osmosis, evaporation, ion exchange and adsorption are utilized to remove lead and other heavy metals [8]. But most of these methods have disadvantages such as high costs, low efficiency, and the production of surplus pollutants [9]. In the past few years, because of its easy to use, low cost, convenient operation, and the proper efficiency and non-production of hazardous side-products, the adsorption procedure has been utilized more than other methods for the elimination of pollutants [10,11]. The most impressive technical and affordable procedures in this field, is the utilization of magnetic nano-particles [12]. Magnetic nanoparticles with a large external layer area, powerful uptake capacity and adsorption rate, have been of interest to researchers for many years. One of their most striking features is that almost all atoms are located on the external layer of the nanoparticles. Consequently, they have the potential to lead to more atoms; as a result, their chemical activity will increase and they will be able to attract more ionic species [13]. Today, the use of graphenic magnetic nanoparticles has attracted many researchers because of their high specific surface and the ability of adsorption [14,15]. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), a single-dimensional allotropy of graphite carbon, has recently been known as a part of the extended family of graphene [16]. GQDs have unique advantages such as adjustable surface finishes, excellent electrical properties and high surface properties, which have made them multi-purpose nanomaterials and have wide applications [17]. GQDs have been utilized in biosensors [18], drug carriers and targeted delivery of nuclear drugs [19,20]. They have also been used as an electrochemical substance in energy conversion and storage [21]. Natural halloysite nanotubes (HNTs) with an approximate diameter of 80 nm and a length of 1 μm have been greatly considered. They are two-layered amino-silicates composed of Al-OH moieties on the internal surface and SiOSi groups on the external surface and have a similar structure to kaolin [22]. Compared to other nanoscale materials, HNTs (Al2Si2O5(OH)4·nH2O) are easily accessible and cost-effective, so as a naturally occurring nano-adsorbent, it can dramatically increase the elimination of metallic ion species by adsorption. The high specified surface, either individually or in composites, can be very efficient as the phase of the filler for the elimination of metallic ions species [23,24]. Recently, extensive researches have been executed on the utilization of magnetic adsorbents with graphenes, for example, magnetic graphene oxide nanohybrids have been utilized to remove lead from aqueous solutions [25]. Some of other adsorbents used for removal of lead (II) are carbon nanotubes [26], CeO2-MoS2 hybrid magnetic biochar [27], Acacia nilotica seed shell ash supported Ni0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4 magnetic nanoparticles [12] and chitosan/alginate/Fe3O4@SiO2 hydrogel composites [28].

The response surface method (RSM) is the statistical model applied in the plan of the experiments, which is a simple, effective, inexpensive, and flexible way to optimize various processes. Another advantage of this procedure is the ability to perform analysis of variance, to conclude the final formula for removal and to control the best theoretical conditions. This procedure could be implemented using the Box-Behnken design (BBD) [29].

Here we first introduce the graphene quantum dots anchored on the external layer of NiFe2O4-HNTs as an impressive adsorptive material. The synthesized magnetic adsorbing material could simply be isolated from the aqueous medium with the help of an applied magnetic field. The adsorption performance of Pb(II) onto this new adsorbing agent has been investigated in detail.

HNT powder was purchased from US Natural Nano Inc. Nickel nitrate hexahydrate, iron(III) nitrate nonahydrate, (3-aminopropyl)-triethoxysilane (APTES), N-(3-(dimethylamino)-propyl)-N′-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC) citric acid and N-hydroxysuccinimde (NHS) were bought from Sigma-Aldrich Inc. Toluene was obtained on an analytical scale without any purification and was used. All the solutions are made of double distilled deionized water. The standard solution of Pb(II) (1000 mg/L) was bought from Merck and diluted as needed.

Egg white (60 mL) was mixed with distilled water (40 mL). Then resultant mixture was agitated severely to entirely mix and make a transparent, steady solution. Afterwards 2.9081 g of nickel nitrate hexahydrate, 8.0800 g of iron(III) nitrate nonahydrate and 0.5 g of halloysite were delivered gradually to the former solution and was agitated severely at ambient temperature for 2 h until the salts were entirely dissolved. Then, while the mixture was stirred, it was heated to 80 °C until dried. The resulting powder was grinned and heated at 700 °C for 3 h. [30].

NiFe2O4-HNTs were coated with APTES by formerly described procedure [31]. In brief, at first, the APTES before adding NiFe2O4-HNTs was dissolved in anhydrous toluene. Then the resulted mixture was dispersed sonochemically for 30 min. Then the consequent mixture was heated at 120 °C for 20 h under constant agitating to achieve the APTES-coated NiFe2O4-HNTs. The solid phase in the consequent mixture was isolated, washed with toluene to eliminate the unreacted APTES, and then dried at 70 °C.

GQDs composed by thermal decomposition of citric acid [32]. In brief, citric acid (0.2 g) was heated at 200 °C for 5 min. Then, the consequent yellow liquid was added gradually in 20 mL of 0.25 M sodium hydroxide solution. Afterwards, the resulted graphene quantum dot solution was kept in 4 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Immobilization of GQDs on APTES-coated NiFe2O4/HNTs surface
ce:section.ce:para: The GQDs immobilized on NiFe2O4-HNTs nanocomposites were prepared by the linking of –COOH groups of GQDs and the –NH2 groups of APTES coated NiFe2O4-HNTs by way of the classic EDC/NHS reaction [33]. Briefly, 10 mL of as-synthesized GQDs solution was charged with EDC (5 mg) and NHS (6 mg) under severe stirring. Afterwards APTES coated NiFe2O4-HNTs were added to the solution and reacted for 4 h under stirring. The consequent solid phase was separated, washed with deionized water to eliminate the unreacted GQDs, and then dried at 70 °C for subsequent use.

Adsorption of lead ions on NiFe2O4/HNTs/GQDs magnetic nanoparticles was investigated under discontinuous conditions. All tests were done in 250 mL Erlenmeyer flasks containing specific amounts of adsorbent and initial concentration of lead ions, at different times. By thinning the Pb(II) solution (1000 mg/L), solutions were obtained with the required concentrations. For the purpose of optimization of the test conditions, the impact of pH on the uptake procedure was studied in the amount of 3 to 6. The initial concentrations for lead were 10, 50 and 100 mg/L, the contact times were 10, 40 and 60 min, and the adsorbing nanomaterial doses were 0.04, 0.2, and 0.4 g. The solutions prepared in accordance with the model presented by the RSM, were mixed at a continuous rate of 200 rpm and the duration set for each test. After the mentioned time, the solutions reached to the equilibrium, at the next step, the NiFe2O4/HNTs/GQDs was isolated by a suitable magnet. Then, the residual concentrations of Pb(II) was assessed by atomic absorption spectroscopy. The adsorption efficiency (% removal) and uptake capacity (qe, mg/g) could be computed from the subsequent equations:
(1)

where qe is equilibrium adsorption capability (mg/g). C0 and Ce are the beginning and equilibrium concentrations (mg/L) of Pb(II) in the solution, respectively. V and m are the volume of solution (L) and mass of adsorbing material (g), respectively.

Isotherm and thermodynamic considerations were conducted by investigating the consequence of thermometric conditions (15–55 °C) and the initial concentrations of (50–500 mg/L). The kinetic survey was executed at optimum concentration achieved from the response surface procedure at the period of 80–100 min at ambient temperature. After that, various parameters of isothermal, kinetical and thermodynamical investigations were analyzed.

The RSM contains many empirical designs that can improve the impact of interacting components on the dependent variable (response). In this report, a four-factor and three-level Box–Behnken design (BBD) was employed utilizing Design-Expert software version 11 to manage the maximum amount of Pb(II) adsorption on NiFe2O4/HNTs/GQDs. The level of confidence for the variables was 95%. The process of optimizing the pattern of the response surface test has four main stages: a) establishing a suitable test design; b) proposing a statistical model connected with regression analysis; c) verifying the suggested model; and d) predicting the response parameter on the basis of proposed model [34,35].

The Box–Behnken design model can be shown as follows:
(3)

where Y is the forecasted reply (% removal); Xi and Xj are the coded amounts of unrelated parameters; and β0, βi, βii, and βij are the regression coefficients for the intercept, linear, squared, and interaction terms, respectively. ε is random error [36,37]. Parameters and their levels are indicated in Table 1 . The empirical data were fitted by a quadratic polynomial equation and the meaning of the model was judged by variance analysis.

The crystalline phase of the prepared nanocomposites was examined by a Philips Company diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.54 Å). The morphologic structure of NiFe2O4/HNTs/GQDs was described by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) with a LEO 912AB (120 kV) Zeiss electron microscope (Germany). Nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherms surveys were implemented on an Autosorb-1 Quantachrome-Sorptometer (USA). The effective surfaces and the pore size distribution were examined using the Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) procedure. FT-IR spectra were registered on a Bruker model 470 spectrophotometer to confirm functionalization of the specimens as KBr pellets. The magnetic specification was examined by vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM, LDJ9600). Elemental compositions were measured with EDS analysis (model 7353, Oxford Instruments, UK), with 133 eV resolution. The concentrations of lead(II) ions were evaluated by an atomic absorption spectrophotometer (Varian AA240FS).

For the synthesis of NiFe2O4/HNTs/GQDs, NiFe2O4-HNTs were constructed by co-precipitation of NiFe2O4 along with the HNTs. Then, for the purpose of connect the GQDs to NiFe2O4-HNTs, the surface was first coated by APTES (APTES coated NiFe2O4-HNTs). Afterwards, GQDs were linked to the external layer of the APTES coated NiFe2O4-HNTs in attending of NHS/EDC (Scheme 1 ).

Since GQDs have carboxylic groups, they react rapidly with –NH2 groups of APTES coated NiFe2O4/HNTs and make an amide bond, causing GQDs firmly attached to the external layer of NiFe2O4-HNTs. The shape of NiFe2O4-HNTs and NiFe2O4/HNTs/GQDs surfaces were assessed by TEM, as shown in Fig. 1a and b, respectively. As has been seen in Fig. 1b, GQDs are dispersed almost uniformly at the NiFe2O4-HNTs surface. The findings are compatible with previous reports [38].

Fig. 2a shows XRD patterns of nano-materials based on NiFe2O4-HNTs. The two main peaks of HNTs can be observed at 2θ = 12, 24.9; the former is referred to as a dehydrated HNT peak, which is ascribed to the reflection peak with the Miller index of 001 and d = 0.74 nm, and it is confirmed by the second peak (Miller index 002) [39]. The mentioned peaks are marked with red arrows. Moreover, the indicator and dominant peaks are all in agreement with standard XRD pattern of NiFe2O4 (JCPDS: 10-0325) [30]. The APTES coated NiFe2O4-HNTs diffraction pattern exhibits the same peaks compared to NiFe2O4-HNTs peaks. This recommends that the APTES link to NiFe2O4-HNTs did not change its crystallinity. Also, in the NiFe2O4/HNTs/GQDs diffraction pattern, no sign of identifying GQDs, which may be the result of their low amounts, high dispersions and low crystallinity of GQDs in NiFe2O4/HNTs/GQDs nanocomposites [38,40].

Changes in the functional groups of nanomaterials based on NiFe2O4-HNT, including NiFe2O4-HNTs, APTES coated NiFe2O4-HNTs and NiFe2O4/HNTs/GQDs, were investigated by FT-IR. As shown in Fig. 2b, in the FT-IR spectrum of the previously generated NiFe2O4-HNTs nanocomposites, a peak detected at 540.61 cm−1 related to AlOSi and a shoulder peak located at 600.23 cm−1, essentially accompanying to the FeO bond vibration in NiFe2O4 can be detected. The presence of NiFe2O4 nanoparticles is confirmed by absorption bands of 600.23 and 436.26 cm−1, which are related to vibrations of FeO and NiO bonds, respectively. The absorption bands located at 1086.49 and 1037.22 cm−1 are specific bands of the SiOH and SiOSi groups, which confirm the attendance of APTES on the NiFe2O4-HNTs surface. The connection of the APTES propyl groups is proven by a stretching vibration of CH at 2929.40 cm−1 [41]. The broad peak appeared in the range of 3350–3440 cm−1 resulting from the participation of the vibrational signals of the hydroxyl (–OH) and amine (–NH2) groups in the APTES sections [38]. The absorption bands observed at 1576.18 and 1625.88 cm−1, correlated to bending vibration of CC and stretching vibration of CO respectively, which is a strong reason for the presence of GQDs on the nano-composite surface [40]. All the above observations point to the proper construction of NiFe2O4/HNTs/GQDs. Fig. 2c shows the N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms of nitrogen and the pores size distribution. All nanomaterials based on NiFe2O4-HNT show an isotherm type IV with hysteresis loop H3 at relative pressure (P/P0) of 0.7–0.95. This kind of isotherm is a considerable feature of capillary density in the mesoporous materials [42]. The specified area of the as-generated NiFe2O4-HNTs was 42.2 m2/g, which is lower than the HNTs surface (61 m2/g). This reduction is principally resulting from the sedimentation of NiFe2O4 nanoparticles on the external layer of the HNTs. After covering with APTES, the specific area again decreased to 27.5 m2/g and the average width of the cavities was reduced from 18.2 nm (for NiFe2O4-HNTs) to 11.3 nm (for APTES-coated NiFe2O4-HNTs). The amino propyl groups presence covers the surface and the pores of as-prepared NiFe2O4-HNTs, which brings about a decrease in the surface and diameter of the cavities. The APTES coated NiFe2O4-HNTs with GQDs reduces the specified area and thickness of the cavities (25.3 m2/g and 8.8 nm respectively), indicating a successful deposition of GQDs. The results are compatible with preceding reports [21,38].
Elemental analysis was performed by EDS (energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy). The outcomes are displayed in Table 2 .

As shown in Table 2, the attendance of carbon, oxygen, aluminum, silicon, iron and nickel in NiFe2O4/HNTs/GQDs is confirmed. The magnetic characteristic of NiFe2O4/HNTs/GQDs was identified by a vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM) examination. As indicated in Fig. 3 , the M(H) hysteresis loop is entirely reversible, which suggests that the NiFe2O4/HNTs/GQDs has superparamagnetic properties. Their hysteresis rings reach the saturation at the highest applied magnetic field. At ambient temperature, the magnetic saturation amount for NiFe2O4/HNTs/GQDs is 60.82 emu/g. These nanoparticles exhibit high permeability in magnetization, and their magnetic properties are so large that they can be separated by a conventional magnet.

In this study, the number of experiments was 27 tests (with three replications), and a total of 27 tests with three replications were designed and performed [43,44]. The results that were observed and predicted by Design Expert software is given in Table 3 .

The positive mark before the parameters mentions the interactive impact of the variable on the model and the negative sign indicates a decreasing effect or the opposite impact on the model. When a raise in the amount of a variable occurs after increasing another, a positive correlation coefficient indicating the synergistic effect occurs and when the decrease of the amount of one variable is accompanied by the declining of the another variable's value, there is a negative correlation coefficient [45].
To confirm the validity of the presented model, analysis of variance, correlation coefficient (R2) value and residual charts were investigated.

Statistical analysis is to evaluate the accuracy and adequateness of the model. ANOVA is a statistical method that examines the significance level and importance of the whole model and each component with probability values of prob. < F < 0.05. The adequacy and predictability of the model was examined using the lack of fit criteria, linear regression coefficient R2, R2 adjust, R2 predicted, adequate precision, and residual detection. Analysis of variance of second order model of the Pb(II) removal process by NiFe2O4/HNTs/GQDs are exhibited in Table 4 . As can be seen, the p-value of the suggested model for the Pb(II) ions adsorption capacity is <0.0001 (with a 95% confidence level). Because this amount is lower than 0.05, it implies that the suggested model is meaningful. Also, p-value exceeding 0.05 for the model's lack of fit is an indicator for verifying the high accuracy of the model in predicting estimated values for each experiment. In other words, meaningless lack of fit states that the gap between actual and expected values is negligible. Typically, p-value smaller than 0.05 suggests the signification of the parameter, while p-value above 0.05, indicates a meaninglessness [45]. F ratio is a critical element in the statistical survey of a model. If the F ratio amount for a factor is high, it implies that the desired factor is important and its consequence on the response rate is more substantial. Therefore, the outcomes of the analysis of variance and the significance test of the regression model are briefed in Table 4. The F value for the model (331.17) is higher than the one in standard distribution table at 95% confidence level, is above the one in standard distribution table at 95% confidence level, which indicates the prominence of the prob. > F value model for a model <0.05 (95% confidence level), which also shows that this model is statistically meaningful. The extent of the F ratio of each one of the elements and interactions indicates the relative magnitude of their impact on the results.

As a consequence of the F ratios for Pb(II) removal results, pH has the greatest impact on the results. After this factor, the elements of adsorbent dose, Pb(II) concentration and time have the greatest impact on the Pb(II) removal results, respectively. The interrelationship among other factors has not been so important.

The large index of correlation (R2 = 0.9974) indicates a correlativity between the observed and forecasted amounts of the model that affirms the authenticity of the model. Also, the adequate precision value equals to 64.0969, which specifies the difference between the predicted amount of the model and the average value of the forecasted error, proves that the model reports the performance of the adsorptive system well. In general, the values above 4 confirm the exactitude of the proposed model [37].

The residual chart and normal probability graph are exhibited in Fig. 4a and b, respectively. The accuracy of model matching was investigated by the residual against the predicted values plot. In this case, all data are presented in the range of ±4 of the residues. Such matching indicates that there is no problem with the proposed model. Also, in the normal diagram 4b, it can be seen that the points in the diagram are close to the direct line, indicating the trueness of the assumption that the experimental data is normal.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of the simultaneous impact of the effective parameters in the Pb(II) uptake operation by NiFe2O4/HNTs/GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: RSM in consolidation with Box-Behnken design is utilized to optimization of the parameters for gaining the largest elimination of Pb(II) by NiFe2O4/HNTs/GQDs. The plots were accomplished for a given pair of parameters while holding other parameters constant. Fig. 5a implies the synergistic impacts of pH and contact time on the lead(II) ions removal. As shown, at each constant pH, removal of Pb(II) enhances with raising contact time and the utmost elimination (92.26%) is acquired at 54 min. Fig. 5b shows the combined impacts of pH and beginning concentration of lead(II) ions on the Pb(II) elimination. The experimental outcomes imply that Pb(II) ions elimination is rising by increasing pH until 6 when the beginning concentration was constant at 50 mg/L. Higher than this concentration (for example >50 mg/L), Pb(II) elimination reduces the accessibility of activated sites for interaction with the adsorbent. Fig. 5c illustrates the interactive impact of pH and adsorption amount on the lead(II) elimination by the NiFe2O4/HNTs/GQDs nanocomposite. It was pointed out that the elimination efficacy enhanced with growing of adsorbent amount at any specified pH value. In high acidic media, H+ ions were likely to compete with Pb2+ and therefore, the lead elimination portion is reduced. Thus, the vast Pb(II) adsorption happening at higher pHs could be ascribed to a reduction in contest between H+ and Pb2+ ions at the same adsorptive site of the adsorbing agent external layers. At a pH > 6, the Pb2+ ions start to hydrolyze and then form a slight quantity of Pb(OH)2 or Pb(OH)3 − species [46]. In comparison to Pb2+ ions, these species are unwanted for adsorption, which reduce adsorption capacity. Consequently, highest adsorption happened at near pH 6.0, and was chosen for all adsorption experiments in the present research. Fig. 5d indicates the interactive interaction of the beginning concentration of lead(II) and reaction time. It proves that at each constant time, the elimination portion rises with increasing beginning concentration of lead(II). The reason for this phenomenon is that at low concentrations of lead, certain adsorbent sites have metal-adsorbing properties, but at high concentrations these sites are saturated, which reduces the absorption efficiency. On the other hand, by increasing the initial concentration of lead, the adsorption rate has increased. In more concentrated solutions, owing to the increase in the number of lead ions, effective collisions with adsorptive sites have increased and this increases the adsorption rate [47]. However, in accordance with Fig. 5d at early times, the amount of lead ions in the solution is high, resulting in the number of effective collisions between lead ions and adsorption sites on a high surface. So the adsorption rate is faster at the initial stages of the process. With the passage of time and adsorption of lead cations on the adsorbent surface, the amount of lead ions in the solution is reduced and more of the active sites on the adsorbent surface are saturated by lead cations and the adsorption rate is reduced. In the interaction of contact time and concentration of lead ions, the highest adsorption occurs at 54 min and the concentration of 91 mg/L. The synergistic consequence of the adsorbing agent amount and the beginning concentration of Pb(II) is shown in in Fig. 5e. In the low adsorbent dosage, the removal efficacy decreases owing to the lack of enough active sites to interact with the lead ions and in high doses, lead ions adsorption at any lead(II) beginning concentration rises.

The interaction effect of the adsorbing agent amount with reaction time and its relationship with the removal percentage of lead(II) is shown in Fig. 5f. As shown, the elimination efficacy grows with enhancing NiFe2O4/HNTs/GQDs amount and time. This is induced by the high specified area of NiFe2O4/HNTs/GQDs nanocomposite provides more accessible adsorptive sites.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synergistic effects of four variables on the lead(II) elimination efficacy
ce:section.ce:para: The perturbation plot is generally utilized to examine the synergistic effects of all parameters simultaneously. In conformity with the Fig. 6 , the impact of all variables (pH, time, concentration and adsorbent dosage) on the lead(II) removal efficacy at a point in the RSM method combined with BBD is observable and comparable. The elimination efficacy grows with raising pH and NiFe2O4/HNTs/GQDs amount. pH is an effectual factor in lead ions adsorption [48,49]. While the increase of Pb(II) concentration and contact time had a small effect on the removal efficiency.

To investigate numerical optimization, the lowest and highest levels should be specified for each input variable while the response is designed to achieve maximum. The response surface method predicted the optimal values using desirability function. The value of each independent variable was determined in the manner that a maximum Pb(II) removal of 97.14% may be accomplished. The best possible amounts of the parameters are: contact time, 54 min; beginning pH, 5.96; NiFe2O4/HNTs/GQDs amount, 0.36 mg and lead beginning concentration ions, 91 mg/L. The tests were performed under optimized situations which is consistent with the forecasted values.

Diverse models were proposed for illustrating and reviewing the adsorption kinetics [50]. In the current research, uptake kinetic information was utilized to ascertain the adsorption velocity of lead(II) on NiFe2O4/HNTs/GQDs by Elovich, quasi-second order and quasi-first order models.

The longitudinal format of the quasi-first-order model is as follows [51]:
(5)

where qe and qt (mg/g) are the equilibration adsorption capacity and at the time t in minute respectively. k1 (min−1) is the velocity constant.

The quasi-second order model is as listed below [52]:
(6)

where, k2 (g/mg·min) is velocity constant. The amounts of qe and k2 can be computed from the t/qt vs t plot.

The Elovich model is generally utilized to delineate the chemisorption approach and is stated with Eq. (7) [53]:
(7)

where α and β represent the initial absorption rate (mg/g·min) and a constant (g/mg), which provides information on the level of covered surface.
The kinetic variables of the models are represented in Table 5 .

The best kinetic model that matches the empirical information is selected based on index of correlation (R2) and calculated uptake capability (qe,cal). As seen in Table 5, the R2 amount for the quasi-second order model is 0.999 and for the quasi-first order is 0.944. It may have concluded that both models have high linear response. Also, the computed adsorption capacity of the quasi-second order model is nearer to empirical amount compared with the quasi-first order model. These outcomes indicate that kinetic data for Pb(II) adsorption on NiFe2O4/HNTs/GQDs are well suited to the quasi-second order model. Therefore, the model suggests that the rate determinant step in the uptake procedure may be chemisorption. From the other point of view, in accordance with the outcomes of Table 5 for the Elovich model, the index of correlation is 0.983, which indicates that the model is well fitted with empirical kinetic information. The appropriateness of the Elovich model also indicated that lead adsorption occurs by virtue of a chemisorption process [53]. The results are consistent with earlier studies [3,54].

Experimental equilibrium data for the lead adsorption on NiFe2O4/HNTs/GQDs have been considered using Langmuir, Freundlich, and Temkin isotherms. The linear equations for these isotherms are represented in Table S1.

Linear diagrams of Langmuir, Freundlich, and Temkin isotherms are displayed in Figs. S1, S2, and S3, respectively. The isotherm variables for each model are obtained from the corresponding graphs and briefed in Table 6 . Additionally, the separation factor, RL, is also calculated for the Langmuir model and is defined as follows [55]:
(8)
R L
=
1
1 +
K L
C 0
where C0 is the beginning concentration of Pb(II) ions (mg/L) and KL (L/mg) is the Langmuir constant. The uptake procedure is assumed to be desirable if 0 < RL < 1.

As the results show, because of the amounts of R2, it can be decided that experimental data are more compatible with Langmuir model in all testing temperatures. Many researchers have confirmed the validness of the Langmuir model for the uptake operation [12,54,56]. Based on Langmuir model, the most adsorption capability (qm) and Langmuir constant (KL) for the adsorption process, were computed within 36.23–46.73 mg/g and 0.361–1.132 L/g respectively. The calculated values for RL in this study are within 0.002–0.075 which suggestive of the high desirability of the uptake process (0 < RL < 1).

The thermodynamic variables like enthalpy (ΔH°), free energy (ΔG°) and entropy (ΔS°) variations and activation energy (Ea) are parameters that control the spontaneity of an adsorption process. A process is spontaneous if its ΔG° decreases with increasing temperature [57]. The temperatures applied in thermodynamic study were 288, 298, 313, and 328 K. The impact of temperature on the Pb(II) uptake on NiFe2O4/HNTs/GQDs was examined and it was revealed that the extent of adsorption grew with rising temperature. The discussed thermodynamic variables are computed by the subsequent equations:
(9)
ln
K d
= ln
q e

− ∆G °
RT
(10)

(11)
∆G ° = ∆H ° − T∆S °
where Kd is equilibration constant, qe is the equilibration adsorption capability of Pb(II) (mg/g), Ce is the equilibration Pb(II) concentration (mg/L), T is the solution temperature (K) and R is the gas constant (8.314 J/mol·K).

The outcomes are displayed in Table 7 . Positive enthalpy change (ΔH°) proves that the operation is an endothermic type and the adsorption amount consolidates the construction of partial chemical processes that exist throughout the adsorption operation [58]. The negative value of free energy change (ΔG°) points out that the uptake process is spontaneous. The reduction in ΔG° value with rising temperature also indicates the spontaneous character of the lead(II) uptake process [59]. Afzali and Fayazi reported that Pb(II) adsorption by magnetic halloysite nanotubes@manganese oxide is endothermic and has a spontaneous nature [3]. The entropy change (ΔS°) is also positive; this proves that in the course of the adsorption operation, there is randomness at the interface of the fluid/solid. Sticking probability, S⁎, of an adsorbate on an adsorbing agent can be computed by a corrected form of an Arrhenius equation related to surface coverage (θ). S⁎ is related to adsorbing agent/adsorbate complex and is a criterion of an adsorbed material capability to stay on an adsorbent infinitely [60] and is described as:
(12)
S ∗

1 − θ

(13)

where θ is the coverage area and Ea is the activation energy.
(14)

where C0 and Ce are Pb(II) beginning concentration and concentration at equilibrium, respectively.

The amounts of the sticking possibility and the activation energy indicated in Table 7 are obtained from the graph of ln(1-θ) vs 1/T (Figure was not indicated). The amount of S⁎ is capable to 2.638 × 10−10, which is nearby to zero. This illustrates that the adsorption operation is chemisorption [61]. The amount of activation energy is a standard to determine whether the adsorption process is of a physical or chemical nature. Low level activation energy (<40 kJ/mol) is a characteristic of the physical adsorption process, while greater activation energy values (>40 kJ/mol) propose a chemical adsorption [62]. The activation energy value obtained for adsorption of lead(II) on NiFe2O4/HNTs/GQDs, (48.231 kJ/mol) exhibits that the adsorption operation is chemisorption.

According to the outcomes acquired in the preceding items that emphasize chemical adsorption, lead adsorption is likely to occur through the following pathway which was displayed in Scheme 2 . The lead(II) ions elimination is likely to take place via the construction of complexes (bidentate and monodentate) between the carboxyl (–COO−) and hydroxyl (–OH) moieties on the external layer of NiFe2O4/HNTs/GQDs and the lead(II) ions.

The adsorptive capability is a great significance variable that implies the performance of an adsorbent. Table 8 compares the greatest adsorptive capability of NiFe2O4/HNTs/GQDs with diverse declared adsorbents for the case of adsorbing Pb(II) [12,54,56,63–65].

In this work, nano NiFe2O4/HNTs/GQDs was synthesized as a novel adsorbing agent successfully. The adsorption performance of as-prepared adsorbent for Pb(II) was studied by batch experiments. The good adsorption performance for Pb(II) of NiFe2O4/HNTs/GQDs was mainly described by the complexation interaction between Pb2+ ions and functional groups. The impact of experimental parameters on the elimination portion was examined by Box-Behnken design (BBD) combined with response surface method. The amount of every independent parameter was determined in a way that a maximum lead removal of 97.14% could be achieved. Experimental information was adapted with various models of isotherms. The Langmuir isotherm was picked as the best isotherm model for explanation of the lead(II) adsorption. The utmost adsorptive capability was computed as 42.02 mg/g at 298 K based on Langmuir model. The Elovich model recommends that the adsorption operation is chemical. The outcomes of the survey of kinetic patterns demonstrated that Pb(II) adsorption on NiFe2O4/HNTs/GQDs follows a quasi-second-order model. The calculated thermodynamic variables exhibited that the uptake operation is heat-absorbing and spontaneous. Therefore, the NiFe2O4/HNTs/GQDs nano-adsorbent affords an economical and efficient way to eliminate Pb(II) ions.
